coredata.dc:title: Machine-Learning driven STM images prediction of doped/defective graphene: Towards optimized tools for 2D nanomaterials characterization
coredata.dc:creator: Guerrero-Rivera, Rubén
Godínez-Garcia, Francisco J.
Hayashi, Takuya
Wang, Zhipeng
Ortiz-Medina, Josué
coredata.dc:description: Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) is a key characterization technique that allows for visualization of specific features at nanostructures, given its ability to reveal changes in local charge densities in doping or defective sites. Besides the experimental acquisition of STM data from real samples, there is the theoretical route, which allows for STM images simulation from ab-initio calculations on defined nanostructures. This work presents an alternative route for theoretical STM image prediction by means of Machine Learning (ML) methods, based on the training of deep convolutional neural networks using simulated STM data. The ML-based STM predictions for defective, B- and N-doped graphene quantum dots are compared to ab-initio STM simulations, in terms of accuracy of structural rearrangements, charge density localizations, and computational resources requirements. The results demonstrate that STM images predicted by ML-methods are accurate at doping sites, whereas they show good similarity to simulations of large structural vacancies. This work represents a novel alternative for the use of ML methods in image-based characterization routines of doped/defective graphene, and potentially, for other 2-dimensional nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Machine learning
STM
Doping
Structural defects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of data flux for the two compared routes for STM images simulation: conventional (top part) vs optimized (bottom part). The conventional route is based on DFT methods for the STM images simulation, whereas the optimized route involves the use of a trained DCNN for a fast prediction of STM images. The DCNN training phase is dependent on original data from DFT calculations.

U-Net architecture representation of the CNN used for STM images prediction. The architecture used involved the processing of input images of 256 x 256 pixels, which are decoded down to squared 128, 64, 32 and 16 pixels, concatenated and encoded back to the original squared 256 pixels size. The figure depicts the classical U-Net feature of direct pathways to complement the encoding phase.

Examples of a) graphene-based structures with vacancies and/or B-, N-heterodoping (white, grey, pink, and blue balls represent H, C, B and N atoms, respectively). b) ab-initio based STM simulation involves a structural relaxation step, where the vacancies (seen as colored areas) and doping sites alter the lattice structure. STM simulated images are produced after the structural relaxation and electronic characteristics determination. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Comparison of simulated (by DFT) and predicted (by ML) STM images of graphene QDs examples. QDs in a) row contains only B- and N- heterodoping sites (pink and blue vertices in left column structures, respectively), whereas b) and c) QDs have vacancies. Note that the molecular structures in left column are relaxed structures, and that the input for STM predictions were initial structures without relaxation (not shown). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Comparison of DFT-calculated (central column) and ML-simulated (right column) STM images from specific B- or N- heterodoping sites (rows a and b), monovacancy (row c), and two divacancy configurations (row d labeled as cross-like divacancy, and row e labeled as bone-like divacancy). The calculated and simulated images are the result of averaging local doping/defective sites at the original images from the dataset. The left column shows the structural model (before any relaxation by DFT calculations) for each local doping/defective site.

Comparison of computing time consumption by the two different approaches (DFT and ML) used for STM images production. The comparatives are shown for two phases: the training and the production phase. The training phase consists of the time spent for STM simulation via DFT, and the training of CNN architecture using the resulting images from DFT calculations, both for 1000 structures. The production phase shows the average time spent for STM simulation (DFT) and prediction (ML) for one single structure.

Mean squared error (MSE), structural similarity index measure (SSIM), and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) for DFT and ML based STM images. The values are compared for heterodopant (B or N) and for three different types of vacancies. The metrics are related to the images shown in Fig. 4.

head.ce:title: Machine-Learning driven STM images prediction of doped/defective graphene: Towards optimized tools for 2D nanomaterials characterization

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: TecNM/Instituto Tecnológico de Durango, Division of Research and Postgraduate Studies, and Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, 1803 Felipe Pescador Blvd., Durango, Dgo 34080, México

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Initiative for Supra-Materials, Shinshu University, Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture 380-8553, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Initiative for Supra-Materials, Shinshu University, Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture 380-8553, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Avenue, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Avenue, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province 330022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of Machine Learning methods as an optimized route for STM images prediction of doped/defective graphene, represents an efficient alternative for quick graphene and other 2D materials research. This work demonstrates the feasibility of using Machine Learning for STM images prediction based on structural information, expanding the applicability of these new computational approaches to the materials science field.

Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) is a key characterization technique that allows for visualization of specific features at nanostructures, given its ability to reveal changes in local charge densities in doping or defective sites. Besides the experimental acquisition of STM data from real samples, there is the theoretical route, which allows for STM images simulation from ab-initio calculations on defined nanostructures. This work presents an alternative route for theoretical STM image prediction by means of Machine Learning (ML) methods, based on the training of deep convolutional neural networks using simulated STM data. The ML-based STM predictions for defective, B- and N-doped graphene quantum dots are compared to ab-initio STM simulations, in terms of accuracy of structural rearrangements, charge density localizations, and computational resources requirements. The results demonstrate that STM images predicted by ML-methods are accurate at doping sites, whereas they show good similarity to simulations of large structural vacancies. This work represents a novel alternative for the use of ML methods in image-based characterization routines of doped/defective graphene, and potentially, for other 2-dimensional nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: We have shared a link to the data used for this research.

ce:section.ce:para: The relevance of materials structuring at the nanoscale has been abundantly justified, in terms of its critical role for catalysis, energy storage, and nanoelectronics applications. The presence of heterodoping and defects in graphene has been thoroughly studied, theoretically and experimentally, for a wide range of promising engineered materials [1–7]. Early works already account for the significant role of such modifications of graphene nanostructure, associating them with important alterations on the charge density distributions at the doping or defect points, and its impact on the surrounding adjacent atoms [5,6]. These changes in the charge density affect the way the nanostructure can interact with surrounding species, which is crucial for catalytic activity [8–10] and enhanced spectroscopy applications [11–13]. The carrier mobility is another feature that is strongly affected by defects and heterodoping sites in graphene, since they represent scattering points where the lattice symmetry is altered and thus, modifies the electronic properties of graphene and related materials. Noteworthy, it has been demonstrated that the presence of defects in graphene can be advantageous. For instance, in spite of the fact that they are detrimental to the carrier mobility of perfect sp 2 carbon lattices, they allow the appearance of a band gap, which is crucial to use these materials as semiconducting devices [14–16].

Given the importance of doping and defect sites in graphene and related nanostructures, their characterization is fundamental for the understanding, and furthermore, the engineering of specific features that pure graphene otherwise would not have. A wide variety of characterization techniques are used for this purpose, including scanning tunneling microscopy (STM), scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM), and the related annular and high-angle annular dark field (ADF and HAADF respectively). These techniques in particular allow for atomic-level visualization of materials, where dislocations, doping, and defect sites could be appropriately characterized. Nevertheless, STM, which is related to the atomic force microscopy (AFM) in setup and general working principle, is quite popular given the relative sample preparation easiness and low cost. STM is based on, as the name implies, the sub-micron scale scanning of the surface of any material under study. The probe samples the local charge density, by measuring the current that tunnels from/to the sample. In 2-dimensional nanostructures such as graphene, these changes in local charge density are observed at doping and defect sites, which has resulted valuable for understanding a wide variety of catalytic, semi- or superconducting, and other physical phenomena [17–20]. Moreover, STM imaging can be simulated, usually by means of common ab-initio methods such as Density Functional Theory (DFT) based codes. The STM simulation has the characteristic of being based on the calculated charge density around each on the atomic sites at the studied structure, following mainly two different theoretical approaches: Tersoff-Hamann [21], or Bardeen [22], among other new proposed methods [23,24]. Most of the DFT-based packages with STM simulation capability are based on Tersoff-Hamann or Bardeen’s approximations, and all of them have the possibility of tuning the STM simulation at some degree, e.g., for specific height (constant distance between the STM probe tip and the sample), or specific current. As mentioned, this combination of experimental and theoretical results increases their significance for catalytic, sensing, and semiconductor engineering at the nanoscale, just to mention a few areas.

The STM simulation has disadvantages related to the ab-initio methods, namely, the high computational resources required to perform accurate electronic properties calculations. In recent years, with the incursion of alternative computational approaches which were usually focused on big-data and computational science, novel applications of artificial intelligence (AI) methods have been showing a tremendous potential as an alternative to classical computational methods. Materials science is not the exception, as recent works report on the use of Machine Learning (ML) methods, which are a subdivision of general AI computational methodologies, for a wide range of studies [25–28]. Noteworthy, many of these works address the usage of descriptive data (i.e., enthalpies, dielectric constants, free energies, optical and electronic band-gaps, etc.) for the training of specialized neural networks (NN) architectures, which afterwards, are capable of predicting such characteristics on new structures. The advantage of ML-based methods is that, after a proper training phase, the computational cost for producing predictions on new materials is considerably lower than the cost associated with usual ab-initio methods.

It is important to highlight that, as mentioned, most of the ML use in materials science is based on descriptive data from large databases (e.g., ANI-1 [29], Materials Project [30], OQMD [31], QM9 [32], DDSE [33], etc.), which are the base for many of the recent publications on the topic. Nevertheless, one of the main strengths of ML methods relies on their capability of processing images, which from our point of view, has been underused so far. A recent review on the topic [34] addresses several applications of ML-based image processing for the identification and classification of metallic, ceramic, and polymeric structures from scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. However, the tasks assigned to ML algorithms are mainly oriented to the quick interpretation and classification of data from experimental microscopy routines. The potential for predicting images is a new area in materials science where ML has just started to be exploited. For instance, in one previous work, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) were trained on bidimensional projections of 3D molecular models, in order to predict electronic band-gaps of organic molecules containing a small set of atomic species [35]. The results were encouraging and demonstrated the potential for using image-based methods in materials science research.

Given these unexplored possibilities, in this work we propose the use of a specific CNN architecture, which can predict STM images of doped and defective graphene-based structures, based on simple structural information (i.e., xyz spatial coordinates). Fig. 1 shows a scheme of the data flux, where the conventional route for ab-initio based STM simulations is shown, and its relationship in terms of data input, training dataset production, and output.

A total of 1200 STM images were generated by means of ab-initio simulations, which were used for training and testing the ML architectures described in this work. The starting point was the generation of 1200 random graphene-like structures, based on a squared graphene unit (QD) with 133C-atoms plus 31H-atoms for passivation at the edges. The dimensions of the unit were around 2.0 x 2.0 nm (see figure S1 in supplementary information). The atoms within the central section of the QD, defined as the inner square 0.5 nm from the edges, were randomly changed by N and/or B atoms, or deleted in order to create one or more vacancies. These atoms removals or replacements were limited to be in between 0 % to 10 % of total atoms in the central area, for each type of doping or vacancy. This procedure was automatically done by a Python script working on typical xyz structure files, until the 1200 different structures of doped/defected graphene QDs were generated.

Next, an ab-initio run was done for each one of the 1200 generated structures. The calculations were carried out using SIESTA DFT code [36]. The generalized gradient approximation within the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof parametrization was used for the functional exchange–correlation implementation. A single-ζ basis set for the atomic orbitals, with a plane wave cutoff energy of 100 Ry was used for all the calculations. For.

the electronic structure calculations, all the molecules were relaxed by conjugate gradient minimization until the maximum forces were lower than 0.06 eV Å−1. The DFT calculations produced valence pseudo charge density (ρ) data which could be used by a SIESTA utility code to produce a STM simulated bitmap, based on the Tersoff-Hamann approximation (simulated at a constant-height = 3.0 Bohr).

Fig. 2 shows the CNN implementation for semantic segmentation. This model is based on the U-Net architecture [37]. As the original U-Net, the network consists of five encoding stages (contraction path) and five decoding stages (expansion path). Each encoding stage is made of two convolutional layers followed by a Max pool layer. On the other hand, each decoding stage utilized a transpose convolution followed by two convolutional layers. As Fig. 2 shows, there are direct links from the encoding to the decoding stages (depicted as straight arrows), these links are implemented as concatenations of encoded with decoded images at each stage. The output of the network applied a SoftMax activation function, to perform a 256-grayscale classification for each pixel by assigning a probabilistic value for each grayscale value [38]. At each encoding stage, the number of filters was decided in an incremental binary fashion, starting from 16 filters up to 256 filters in the last decoding stage. Conversely, the decoding path decreases the number of filters in the same fashion, ending up with 16 filters in the last decoding stage. All convolutional.

layers were implemented using a 3 x 3 filter, a padding = ‘same’, and a ‘ReLU’ as the activation function. Similarly, the Max pool layer parameters were: Pool size = 2, Padding = ‘same’, & Stride = 2. Transpose convolutions utilized the same parameters as the Max pool layer. Additionally, an intermediate 20 % dropout layer between consecutive convolutional layers was inserted to apply regularization and avoid overfitting of the network during training as well as to improve computational throughput [39].

1000 out of the 1200 generated images were used exclusively for training and the remaining 200 for the testing phase. All images were separated into red, green and blue (RGB) components prior to their input, and were resized to 256 x 256 pixels to facilitate image matching from the contraction path to the expansion path since 256 is a multiple of 2n, so the effect of the Max pool operation (down-sampling) as well as the transpose convolution (up-sampling), implies halving or doubling the image. The optimizer used to train the model was the Adam algorithm, given its characteristics of computation and memory efficiency [40], using the mean squared error (MSE, see following section) as the loss function metric. For this task, a total of 27 epochs were performed to train the network, yielding a validation loss = 4.23, in contrast to a training loss of 4.15.

Prediction of STM images was directly performed over the trained CNN algorithm. The procedure consists of using images of 2D graphene QDs molecular structures as input, which were automatically created for the 1200 structures analyzed by the Mogli library for Python (see figure S2 in supplementary information). This visual information was used as input to the trained CNN, which processed the images and produced a STM image prediction for each of the input structures as output.

The typical MSE calculation was used for quantitative assessment of differences between predicted and calculated STM images. The MSE was calculated as follows:
MSE =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
x i
-
x i
^
2
where n is the number of pixels in a single image, x is the pixel intensity value (0–255) in the simulated STM image, and x̂ is the pixel intensity value (0–255) in the predicted STM image. In this way, the MSE represents a pixelwise-based error. Alternatively, we used a couple of standardized metrics for image similarity assessment: the structural similarity index measure (SSIM) [41], which is calculated as follows:
SSIM
( x , y )
=
2
μ x
μ y
+
c 1
2
σ
xy
+
c 2
μ
x
2
+
μ
y
2
+
c 1
σ
x
2
+
σ
y
2
+
c 2
where µ stands for pixel samples mean values (either for x or y image), σ 2 stands for variances, σ represents the covariance of the images, and c stands for variables which considers the dynamic range of pixel values. And the Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) [42], whose value is obtained according to the equation:
PSNR = 10 ∙
log
10
I
max
2
MSE
where Imax is the maximum possible value of intensities in the image, (in our case Imax = 255), and MSE is the mean square error between the predicted image and the simulated STM images. Since the PSNR is computed as a logarithmic scale, this allows for a wider dynamic range of values where the higher the value, the higher the quality of the predicted image [43]. Both the SSIM and PSNR measurements were performed by predefined routines in the Scikit-image library, implemented in Python.

For a performance comparison between ab-initio and ML methods, a simple quantification of the required time for each type of computation was done. The comparison was carried out by dividing the process in two phases: training and production. The training phase included not only the computational time consumed during the CNN training, but also, the consumed time by SIESTA code to process and generate the 1000 STM simulated images used for NN training. On the other hand, the production time was considered to be the computational time spent either by the DFT code or the trained CNN to process one structure. The computer used for DFT calculations was based on an AMD Ryzen 7 octacore microprocessor (3.7 GHz clock speed), with 16 GB DDR4 RAM, while a Tesla T4 (using an Intel Xeon with 2.3 GHz clock speed) processing unit with 16 GB of reserved RAM, as normally implemented in Google Colab notebooks.

This work intends to assess the feasibility of ML methods as an efficient alternative to conventional DFT-based calculations for STM imaging simulations. To do so, DFT-based STM images were generated from random graphene-like structures (graphene QDs), with vacancies and B- and/or N-heterodoping. Fig. 3 depicts examples of molecular structures which were used for STM image simulation and prediction (see also the scheme for «conventional route» in Fig. 1). Fig. 3b specifically describes the process of structural relaxation, which aims to find a molecular structure in its energy minimum in terms of its interatomic forces. This configuration is subsequently used for the final electronic structure determination (based on the DFT approximation) and for the corresponding STM image simulation. It is necessary to highlight this relaxation process since, in fact, it is part of the high time-consumption nature of DFT, and other ab-initio based computational calculations.

In the case of graphene QDs, where defects at the sp 2 carbon lattice occur, the structural relaxation leads to local rearrangements of carbon atoms, visible through the appearance of non-hexagonal (pentagons, heptagons, etc.) This can be observed in Fig. 3b, where the shape of the initial vacancies produced by the removal of carbon atoms (see green-shaded areas) from the lattice, changes by the formation of surrounding pentagons in the relaxed structure (blue-shaded areas). These structures are finally the ones that match the STM simulations and the experimental observations.

Fig. 4 shows a comparison of STM images from DFT-based simulations, and from ML-based predictions. Note that the figure shows, in the left and center columns, the atomic relaxed structure and simulated STM image, respectively, of graphene QDs examples. These structures and STM simulations were obtained by means of DFT calculations, whereas the left column depicts the results of STM prediction by the trained.

CNN model. One crucial finding is that, after proper training, the used U-Net architecture for ML-based model was able to, at some degree, correctly predict the atomic structural rearrangements that are associated with the geometric relaxation step which, as discussed, contributes to the high time and computational resources used during a conventional DFT calculation. For instance, it can be seen in Fig. 4a (top row), that, in the case of a QD with B- and N- heterodoping sites, the STM prediction is remarkably.

precise, in terms of intensity differences representing charge density variations. In the other hand, Fig. 4b and 4c (middle and bottom rows) show cases with vacancies, and an associated structural rearrangement. In these cases, the ability of the ML algorithm to predict the correct structure seems to decrease in function of the number of adjacent vacancies. For example, the three defect sites in Fig. 4b are monovacancies (i.e., one C atom removed from the lattice). These defects usually produce the formation of one.

pentagon and one «boomerang» shaped vacancy, which are clearly seen at the relaxed structure and the simulated STM. Noteworthy, STM prediction adequately matches the simulation. Nevertheless, in the case of a QD with vacancies produced by two or more missing C atoms (Fig. 4c), ML results present noticeable mismatches with STM simulations.

We wanted to further study the capabilities of the ML algorithm to replicate individual types of features in predicted STM images, by comparing the similarities between DFT calculated and ML simulated STM images at specific doping or defected sites in graphene. Fig. 5 displays a visual comparison of B- and N-substitutional heterodoping, monovacancy, and two different modes of divacancy (labeled as cross-like divacancy and bone-like divacancy), which can also be numerically contrasted in Table 1. . From the analysis, it is clear that the difference between DFT and ML-based images for B- and N-heterodoping sites, is almost negligible, since their SSIM values are close to 1, whereas the vacancies are predicted by the ML algorithm with larger divergences when compared to DFT-based STM calculations. This can be visually observed in Fig. 5, where it is evident how the ML resembles accurately the charge redistribution produced by the B-/N-dopant atoms. In contrast, vacancies pose a more difficult case for STM image prediction, visible as mismatches between the restructuring of the graphene sp 2 hexagonal lattice towards the formation of pentagons surrounding the vacancy site. This failure in the ML predictions can be seen as darker areas within the vacancy surrounding atoms, compared to the DFT simulated images. These darker areas reveal a lower presence of bonds between peripheral atoms, which form after the structural relaxation process (as part of DFT calculations). Note that STM images, either calculated by DFT or simulated by ML, represent the average of the individual occurrences at the graphene QDs within the dataset. Thus, the lighter shadow-like colors between vacancy bordering atoms reveal the formation of bonds in some of the averaged structures. According to the images, the ML algorithm produces unbalanced bond formation in its STM predictions; for instance, note that Fig. 4c shows a difference between calculated and predicted STM images, particularly in the bone-like vacancy structure. Nevertheless, it should be noted that for.

simple monovacancies, the ML performance is remarkable, in terms of the formation of the associated pentagon and geometric rearrangement towards a boomerang-like defect (compare STM images in Fig. 4b).

The STM images prediction results are, however, possible to improve, given the flexibility of CNN architectures tunning. The nature of CNN models can be practically adjusted, in terms of parameters such as number of layers, activation functions, filter types and sizes, strides, number of training epochs, learning rate, etc. Each one of these parameters has a particular effect on the CNN prediction performance, and it has to be tuned accordingly for a specific type of input. Figure S3 (in supplementary information) shows an example of the features which are extracted from the input images of graphene QDs, which are abstracted during the training phase in order to «learn» the patterns, in terms of charge redistribution and structural modifications. This tuning process is somewhat similar to the selection of parameters for DFT runs (e.g., energy tolerances, mesh cut-offs, k-points meshing, geometrical relaxation step size, convergence algorithm, etc.), and needs at some point of experience gained by trial-and-error experiments. Furthermore, there are alternatives of ML methods which could be more suitable for imaging predictions as described in here, as the Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), which are part of our future research directions.

It is worth to emphasize, nevertheless, the relevance of the findings we just described. Most of the works which use ML methods applied to microscopy imaging are intended for the quick characterization of a large set of experimental results, not for simulating or predicting images. For instance, an interesting work proposes ML methods for predicting the energy of systems in function of the presence and nature of atomic grain boundaries, taking their atomic structure as an input [44]. Another recent paper goes further by predicting crystalline structures, by using ML-based element substitution within cells from a crystals database [45]. We also found an interesting approach which uses CNN in combination with AI techniques called evolutionary methods, intended to the prediction of 2D nanostructures from stochiometric inputs [46]. Noteworthy, this latter paper proposes the use of DFT routines as a part of the main algorithm, in a similar way we are reporting in this work. Nevertheless, in contrast with all these reports, our approach uses DFT only at the initial stage for our in-house produced STM images database. The advantage of this methodology is that, as it will be discussed next, the time consumption performance of ML-based predictions is much higher compared to the DFT-based calculations. This would be attractive in specific for studies which look for a quick understanding of doping/defect engineering and characterization on 2D materials such as graphene.

Finally, we would like to discuss the performance of our proposed ML method, in terms of computational time consumption for the training and production phases (as described in the methodology section). Fig. 6 displays this computing time comparison between DFT and ML algorithms run, for each phase. Note that, in the case of training, the 1700 h spent by the DFT routine included a geometric relaxation for each of the 1000 generated structures and the actual calculation of the electronic structure. The fact that a large portion of the time spent by DFT calculations is dedicated to the geometrical rearrangement of atoms within the structure, until reaching a minimum energetic level, becomes relevant from the results shown by our proposed ML alternative: the STM predicted images, starting from «unrelaxed» molecular structures, show the expected features which result from geometric relaxation, but in a fraction of the time spent by DFT approaches. As Fig. 6 demonstrates, the average time consumed, in production phase, for one STM image by DFT is around 1.7 h, whereas ML takes only 0.8 s, which is three orders of magnitude faster. This reduced time consumption would undoubtedly be advantageous for researchers and nanotechnologists which look for theoretical insights of experimental STM results. The overall evidence we found, therefore, supports our proposal of an efficient alternative for STM images simulation/prediction by ML-based methods.

STM imaging, and its related characterization techniques, are now fundamental for a deep understanding of the physical phenomena occurring at the nanoscale. This is especially the case for new 2D layered materials, which are finding their practical niche in new nanoelectronic and semiconducting technologies. Research on doping and defect engineering of 2D materials such as graphene and other laminar materials like TMDs or MXenes, depend considerably on characterization techniques at the atomic level, with STM and other related methods being a crucial part of these techniques. In this work, we demonstrated that ML-based algorithms can produce, in an efficient way, STM image predictions with an accuracy that allows for a useful complement to experimental results. Moreover, this work opens new routes for developments of computational science approaches towards innovative research in materials science.

Rubén Guerrero-Rivera: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Francisco J. Godínez-Garcia: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Takuya Hayashi: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Zhipeng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Josué Ortiz-Medina: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J. O-M. thanks the financial support of the National Council of Science and Technology (CONAHCYT, México) through the grant CBF2024-2884.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.commatsci.2024.113076.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

T.O. Wehling, K.S. Novoselov, S. V Morozov, E.E. Vdovin, M.I. Katsnelson, A.K. Geim, A.I. Lichtenstein, Molecular Doping of Graphene, Nano Lett 8 (2007) 173–177. http://dx.doi.org/10.1021/nl072364w.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning of Charge Densities in Graphene by Molecule Doping

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of molecular doping of graphene: A first-principles study
